Analytic functions

A remarkable geometrical property enjoyed by all complex
          analytic functions is that,
          at non-critical points, they preserve angles, and
          therefore define conformal mappings.

Examples

Now, with the help of Theorem 1, it is relatively easy to determine
          where an analytic function is a conformal mapping.

[[strong]]Example 1: [[/strong]]
            The mapping @@math:193:da3cbc@@ is conformal throughout the entire @@math:194:cab0ba@@

            mapping @@math:200:cfee4f@@ are a positively
            oriented circle centred at the origin and a ray from
            the origin, respectively. As illustrated
            in Figure 7, the angle between the
            lines at their point of intersection
            is a right angle in the negative direction,
            and the same is true of the angle between
            the circle and the ray at the corresponding point in the @@math:201:bb3e24@@ plane.

A mapping that preserves the magnitude of the angle
          between two smooth curves but not necessarily the sense
          is called an [[em]]isogonal mapping[[/em]].

[[strong]]Example 3: [[/strong]]

The Riemann Mapping Theorem

In many applications it is common to look for a conformal
          mapping of a domain @@math:344:289bf9@@ onto a simpler domain @@math:345:9e3613@@
          A natural question to ask is whether such a mapping exists.
          That is, given domain @@math:346:c245d4@@ and @@math:347:f24d67@@, does there exist a conformal
          mapping of @@math:348:53c99a@@ onto @@math:349:418e62@@?

An answer to this question was given by Bernhard Riemann in 1851.
          Although there was a gap in
          Riemann's original proof (which was subsequently filled), this amazing
          theorem still bears his name:

From this result we see that if @@math:355:e281f1@@ and @@math:356:df87a0@@ are any two simply connected domains,
          with @@math:357:3bd1c8@@ @@math:358:d60c1f@@ then there exists a bijective conformal map @@math:359:839a06@@
          If @@math:360:521490@@ and @@math:361:7a94f8@@ are conformal, we can set @@math:362:00fd78@@
          (see Figure 10). Two domains @@math:363:b2eb56@@ and @@math:364:77855f@@ are called [[em]]conformally equivalent[[/em]]
          if there is a bijective conformal map from @@math:365:b26ee0@@ to @@math:366:089d42@@ Thus the
          [[em]]Riemann Mapping Theorem[[/em]] implies that two simple connected domains (unequal to @@math:367:5075f1@@)
          are conformally equivalent.

Riemann's theorem is of critical theoretical importance, but its proof is
          not constructive. This means that the theorem establishes the existence
          of the mapping @@math:372:22dcf5@@ but it does not offer
          a method to find a formula for @@math:373:68a9d8@@ A proof of
          the [[em]]Riemann Mapping Theorem[[/em]] is beyond the scope of this text.
          The interested reader can refer to 
          the books:

[[em]]Complex Analysis[[/em]]
            by Lars V. Alfors, McGraw-Hill, 1979; and

[[em]]Basic Complex Analysis[[/em]]
            by Jerrold. E. Marsden & Michael J. Hoffman, W. H. Freeman and Company, 1999.
